content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration     Executive/Legal     Enforcement     Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                         322-5901            322-6641        322-6644

October 25, 1977

77-10-072


Melvyn J. CoBen
817 J Street
Post Office Box 1528
Sacramento, California 95807

Dear Mr. CoBen:

Thank you for your letters of October 6, 1977, and
October 13, 1977, requesting an opinion from the Commission on behalf of your client, Firefighters Local 522.
The following staff advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b). Our advice is based on the
following assumed facts.

The Retirement Hearing Commission of the City of
Sacramento has seven members. Section 389 of the
Sacramento City Charter provides that two members shall
be the city manager and the city controller or their
designees, one member shall be a representative of the
city civil service board, three members shall be city
employees, including one employee of the fire department
and one employee of the police department, and the final
member shall be a resident of Sacramento not connected
with city government. The Retirement Hearing Commission
sits as an appeals board from decisions of administrative
law judges granting or denying employee applications for
service retirement, ordinary disability retirement or
industrial disability retirement.

You indicate that an officer of a recognized public
employee organization has been elected by the organization's
members to serve on the Retirement Hearing Commission. The
employee organization pays the elected member a salary of
$75 a month and reimburses him for expenses. The total
received by the member from the organization, therefore,
exceeds $250 a year. The member is not paid a salary or
reimbursed for expenses for the time he spends at Commission meetings.

Melvyn J. CoBen
Page Two

October 25, 1977

You ask whether the proposed conflict of interest code
of the City of Sacramento would require the member to dis-
qualify himself from hearing an appeal in the following
situation:

Assume an applicant for retirement comes
before the Commission seeking to be classified
as an industrial disability while the Retirement
System Manager claims the applicant is eligible
for either an ordinary disability or no retirement.
(Please note, industrial disability pays approxi-
mately 50% of the last 3 years for life and
ordinary disability only 25%). Assume that the
applicant is a member of the same Employee
Organization as the Commission Member and that
the Employee Organization is providing all or
part of the cost of the applicant's appeal to the
Commission by paying such things as attorneys
fees or costs.

We will answer your question by reference to Government
Code Sections 87100 and 87103, which are substantially the
same as the disqualification provisions of the city's pro-
posed conflict of interest code, and which apply to members
of the Retirement Hearing Commission even without the code.

Section 87100 provides:

No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

Section 87103, in relevant part, provides:

An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distin-
guishable from its effect on the public generally,
on:...

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or

Melvyn J. Cohen
Page Three                                October 25, 1977


promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is
made

     Because the employee organization is a source of income
of greater than $250 a year to the Commission member, there-
fore, he may not make, participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence a
decision which foreseeably will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the employee organization.  Under the facts
you have presented, however, we believe that disqualification
would not be required because it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the employee organization will be impacted in a material
way because of individual appeals decisions the Commission
makes.

     For purposes of this advice, we are assuming that the
typical appeals decision does not present an opportunity for
establishing a generalized rule or precedent which would
have an impact beyond the facts of the particular case being
decided.  If such a precedential effect was anticipated, our
advice might be different.  But so long as the decision made
by the Commission will affect only the individual employee
applicant, no disqualification is required.

     We reach this decision because we are not aware of any
way in which an employee organization can be financially
affected by a decision that will affect the employee benefits
received by only one of the organization's members.  Moreover,
there is no evidence that such a decision will have an effect
on the amount of income received by the Commission member
or that there is any nexus between the decision and the pur-
pose for which the member is paid by the employee organization.
See 2 Cal.Adm. Code Section 18702(3).

     It may well be argued that officers of public employee
organizations which sit on the Retirement Hearing Commission
may be biased.  However, the apparent or even likely bias
presented results from the Commission member's status as a
representative of the labor organization.  The possibility of
such bias was apparently taken into consideration when the
Retirement Hearing Commission was established because three
members are required by city ordinance to be labor representa-
tives.  This kind of bias, without any material financial
effect upon the official's source of income, is not the kind
prohibited by the Political Reform Act.

Melvyn J. CoBen
Page Four                                                    October 25, 1977


     You indicate in your letter of October 6, that the labor
organization may be providing all or part of the cost of the
applicant's appeal to the Commission by paying such things as
attorneys' fees or costs. To the extent that the labor
organization will incur significant increased costs because
of an adverse decision of the Retirement Hearing Commission,
it may be necessary for the Commission member to disqualify
himself from participation in the decision regarding the
applicant's eligibility. This issue appears not to be raised
in your letter, however, since the cost of appeal is specifi-
cally limited to the cost of appeals to the Commission, and
does not include appeals from an adverse decision of the
Commission.

     I trust that the foregoing will prove helpful in
resolving the issues presented in your letter. If you have
further questions, please feel free to contact me.

                                                Sincerely,

                                                Michael J. Baker
                                                Chief
                                                Legal Division

MJB:nc

cc  James Jackson
    Sacramento City Attorney

LAW OFFICES OF

MELVYN J. COBEN
817 J STREET
P.O. BOX 1528
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807
Telephone (916) 442-1827

ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICE
SUITE 282
1005 6th STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

TELEPHONE: (916) 441-2613

C. F. "LARRY" QUIRK
ADMINISTRATOR/CONSULTANT

October 13, 1977

REFER ALL MAIL TO:
P.O. BOX 1528
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807


Del Spurlock
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K St.
Sacramento, CA  95814

     RE:  Conflict of Interest Code

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

     In reviewing my letter of October 6, 1977, copy enclosed, my clients
the Fire Fighters as well as Alfred Jackson, Jim Jackson, who I have discussed
the letter with, would like the sixth paragraph of my letter to be reworded
as follows:, "The question we would like answered is as follows:, 'Assuming
an officer of the Recognized Employee Organization of the City of Sacramento
is elected to the Retirement Hearing Commission.' Assuming further, the
Employee Organization does not reimburse the elected member for expenses
relating to Commission hearings nor does the Employee Organization pay the
elected member a salary for Commission meetings but the Employee Organization
does reimburse the elected member for actual out-of-pocket expenses such
as meals and transportation and pays a salary of $75.00 a month to the
elected member but both said reimbursement and salary related to Union
activities not his Commission activities and said reimbursment or salary
totals $250.00 or more in a calendar year.'"

     Both the Employee Organization and the City Attorney believe this
amendment makes the hypothetical much closer to what really occurs.

     As I indicated to you in my earlier letter, this matter is to be
returned to the City Council October 25, 1977 and in Jackson, the City
Attorney, indicates that the very latest date it can go to the Council,
is 90 days from August 9, 1977 or approximately November 8, 1977. Thus
it is important that we have your opinion back before October 25, 1977.

     Further, the City Attorney indicated to me and asked me to communicate
to you that he anxiously awaits your opinion and once having received it,
anticipates writing a concurring opinion and therefore he would appreciate
a response as much before October 25th as possible.


                                                              16

Page 2
Del Spurlock
October 13, 1977

     Please let me know if I may be of any assistance.

                                        Yours truly,


                                        MELVYN J. COBEN

MJC:ed

LAW OFFICES OF

MELVYN J. COBEN
817 J STREET
P.O. BOX 1528
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807
Telephone (916) 442-1827

ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICE
SUITE 202
1005 8N STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

TELEPHONE: (916) 441-2613

C. F. "LARRY" QUIRK
ADMINISTRATOR/CONSULTANT

October 6, 1977

REFER ALL MAIL TO:
P.O. BOX 1528
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95807

Del Spurlock
1100 K St.
Sacramento, CA 98514

RE: Conflict of Interest Code

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

On October 4, 1977 I met with the City Council, City of Sacramento
and they put the matter of the Conflict of Interest Code over to October
25, 1977. In the meantime, recognized employee organizations of the City
of Sacramento were directed to meet with Jim Jackson of the City Attorneys'
Office, City of Sacramento to propose amendments to the Code.

The "Employee Organizations" have a problem primarily with the
application of Section 600 of the proposed Code as it might apply to the
Retirement Hearing Commission.

For your information, the Retirement Hearing Commission is composed
of 7 members. Three members are elected by employee groups (police, fire
and miscellaneous); three members are fundamentally management representa-
tives and the seventh member is an appointment from the Civil Service
Commission.

The function of the Retirement Hearing Commission is to act as an
Appeals Board from the decision of Administrative Law Judges who have
rendered adverse opinions granting or denying employees applications for
service retirement; ordinary disability retirement; or industrial disa-
bility retirements.

Per our earlier discussions with you as the Attorney for the Fire
Fighters Local 522, one of the recognized employee organizations, I am
requesting an opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission pursuant
to Government Code Section 83114. As back up material, I am sending you
Sacramento City Charter Article 24 which establishes the Retirement System
effective January 1, 1977 and Sacramento City Ordinance 3921 which implements
that Article.

The question we would like answered is as follows: "Assuming an
Officer of the recognized Employee Organization of the City of Sacramento
is elected to the Retirement Hearing Commission. Assuming further, the
Employee Organization reimburses the elected member for actual out-of-
pocket expenses, such as meals and transportation of salary resulting
in a total reimbursement of $250.00 or more in a calendar year.

Assume an applicant for retirement comes before the Commission seeking
to be classified as an industrial disability while the Retirement System
Manager claims the applicant is eligible for either an ordinary disability

Page 2
Del Spurlock
October 6, 1977


or no retirement.  (Please note, industrial disability pays approximately
50% of the last 3 years for life and ordinary disability only 25%).
Assume that the applicant is a member of the same Employee Organization as
the Commission Member and that the Employee Organization is providing all
or part of the cost of the applicant's appeal to the Commission by paying
such things as attorneys fees or costs.

Must the Commission Member disqualify himself under Section 600
of the proposed Code or any other Section from voting on the applicant's
appeal?  If the answer to this hypothetical question is in the affirmative,
can the proposed Code be amended to be consistent with the Government Code
in order to allow the Commission Member to vote under these circumstances?

Your response prior to the October 25, 1977 date referred to above,
would be most appreciated.  If I may be of any assistance, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,


MELVYN J. COBEN

MJC:ed
encls.

sections.question: Assuming an Officer of the recognized Employee Organization of the City of Sacramento is elected to the Retirement Hearing Commission. Assuming further, the Employee Organization reimburses the elected member for actual out-of-pocket expenses, such as meals and transportation or salary, resulting in a total reimbursement of $250.00 or more in a calendar year. Assume an applicant for retirement comes before the Commission seeking to be classified as an industrial disability while the Retirement System Manager claims the applicant is eligible for either an ordinary disability or no retirement. Assume that the applicant is a member of the same Employee Organization as the Commission Member and that the Employee Organization is providing all or part of the cost of the applicant's appeal to the Commission by paying such things as attorneys fees or costs. Must the Commission Member disqualify himself under Section 600 of the proposed Code or any other Section from voting on the applicant's appeal?
sections.conclusion: Because the employee organization is a source of income of greater than $250 a year to the Commission member, therefore, he may not make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a decision which foreseeably will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the employee organization. For the facts you have presented, however, we believe that disqualification would not be required because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the employee organization will be financially affected because of individual employees' decisions the Commission makes.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a member of a city retirement hearing commission who receives over $250 annually from an employee organization must disqualify himself from hearing an appeal by a member of that same organization, particularly when the organization is funding the appellant's legal costs.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that disqualification is not required because while the financial interest test is met, an individual retirement benefits decision affecting only one employee will not have a material financial effect on the employee organization. However, disqualification might be required if the decision would establish precedential rules with broader impact or if the organization incurs significant additional costs from an adverse decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letters (October 6 and 13) and FPPC response letter (October 25, 1977). Extracted from FPPC response letter dated October 25, 1977. Some text quality issues due to document scanning/OCR, but key sections identifiable. Question and conclusion sections found within response letter body rather than labeled sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Assuming an Officer of the recognized Employee Organization of the City of Sacramento is elected to the Retirement Hearing Commission. Assuming further, the Employee Organization reimburses the elected member for actual out-of-pocket expenses, such as meals and transportation or salary, resulting in a total reimbursement of $250.00 or more in a calendar year. Assume an applicant for retirement comes before the Commission seeking to be classified as an industrial disability while the Retirement System Manager claims the applicant is eligible for either an ordinary disability or no retirement. Assume that the applicant is a member of the same Employee Organization as the Commission Member and that the Employee Organization is providing all or part of the cost of the applicant's appeal to the Commission by paying such things as attorneys fees or costs. Must the Commission Member disqualify himself under Section 600 of the proposed Code or any other Section from voting on the applicant's appeal?

CONCLUSION: Because the employee organization is a source of income of greater than $250 a year to the Commission member, therefore, he may not make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a decision which foreseeably will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the employee organization. For the facts you have presented, however, we believe that disqualification would not be required because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the employee organization will be financially affected because of individual employees' decisions the Commission makes.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a Retirement Hearing Commission member who receives over $250 annually from an employee organization need not disqualify himself when hearing an appeal from a member of that organization, because individual case decisions affecting only one employee do not create a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the organization itself.
